q: Surface tension is the tendency of a liquid surface to behave like a rigid solid layer.
Surface tension arises from repulsive forces between liquid molecules.
Cohesion refers to attractive forces between molecules of the same substance.
Cohesion causes water molecules to spread out, thereby reducing surface tension.
A water strider is able to sink due to the high density of its legs.
Objects like paperclips float on water because their density is lower than water.
Surface tension is defined as the force per unit area acting on a liquid surface.
The formula for surface tension is $\gamma = Fl$.
The SI unit for surface tension is the Joule per meter.
Surface tension cannot be referred to as a force because it's a tension.
Capillary action is the non-spontaneous flow of a liquid into a narrow tube.
Capillary action is solely caused by cohesive forces within the liquid.
Adhesion refers to forces between molecules of the same substance.
Cohesion and adhesion work against each other in capillary action, preventing liquid from rising.
For wicking to occur, a tube's diameter needs to be very large.
Capillary action is also known as the rise or depression of a liquid in a wide tube.
The rise of water underneath the ground is an example of evaporation.
Water rising into plant roots is an illustration of the Magnus effect.
When a glass tube is dipped in water, the water level within the tube drops below the outside level.
In capillary action, a larger bore tube will result in a higher water rise.
Liquids that 'wet' the tube are depressed within thin tubes.
Mercury is a liquid that wets glass capillary tubes.
Capillarity can occur for non-wetting liquids, but to a lesser extent.
The adhesion between glass and water causes a thick film of water to be drawn up the tube.
Water rises in a capillary tube because adhesive force is less than cohesive force.
The height to which a liquid is lifted in a capillary tube is independent of the contact angle.
The height $h$ of liquid rise in a capillary tube is directly proportional to the density of the liquid $\rho$.
The height $h$ of liquid rise in a capillary tube is inversely proportional to the radius $r$ of the tube.
